headline: Scientists Discover How Light Can Control Magnetism in Ultra-Thin Materials
meta_description: Researchers at City College of New York reveal how light and magnetism interact in atomically thin materials, opening doors to quantum devices and optical memo…
summary_short: A quantum leap: scientists show light can now control magnetic states in materials just a few atoms thick, enabling revolutionary quantum technologies.
body:
At City College of New York, physicists have uncovered something remarkable: in certain ultra-thin materials, light and magnetism are no longer independent forces. They dance together.

When light strikes these atomically thin crystals, it creates excitons—pairs of energized electrons and positively charged holes that remain linked. In ordinary materials, these excitons would simply sit on top of the magnetism. But in van der Waals magnetic semiconductors like chromium triiodide, something different happens. The excitons and magnetic moments emerge from the same place within the material's electronic structure, allowing them to influence each other directly.

"An exciton is not just a passive light-driven excitation," explains Pratap Chandra Adak, lead author of a new review in Nature Materials. "It can sense the spin order and even help control the magnetic state itself."

This breakthrough matters because it opens practical doors. Researchers now see paths toward advanced optical memory, quantum devices that convert microwave signals to light, and magneto-optic lasers. The work, supported by DARPA and the Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation, represents the field moving from simply detecting magnetism in thin crystals to actively controlling it with light.

Many mysteries remain unsolved, but the direction is clear: light and magnetism are partners waiting to be fully understood.
